does: This paper systematically investigates the reliability of evaluation benchmarks for ChatGPT, focusing on data contamination as a critical threat to valid assessment. The authors conduct a detailed case study on stance detection, demonstrating that ChatGPT may have been exposed to benchmark datasets during its training or RLHF phase, which inflates its apparent performance on standard evaluations. They analyze how continuous model updates and the proprietary nature of ChatGPT's training data make it difficult to verify whether test sets remain truly held-out, and they quantify the extent to which contamination affects reported performance metrics.
connects: This work builds on longstanding concerns about data leakage in machine learning evaluation, extending classical contamination problems to the era of large language models trained on internet-scale text. It connects to recent work examining training data memorization and exposure in LLMs, as well as broader literature on benchmark gaming and the reliability of published benchmarks. The paper also engages with challenges posed by RLHF and model updates, building on concerns raised about the opacity of continuously evolving closed-source systems. It relates to prior efforts to detect and measure train-test overlap, adapting those methods to ChatGPT's unique setting of unknown training data and algorithmic updates.
enables: This work points toward developing contamination-aware evaluation protocols for closed-source language models and highlights the need for new benchmarking practices in an era of proprietary, continuously trained systems. It motivates research into detecting dataset exposure without access to training data, potentially through techniques like membership inference or stylistic analysis. The findings support moves toward open-source models with transparent training data, public evaluation on held-out benchmarks, and versioned model releases with clear update notes. It also enables future work on designing evaluation frameworks robust to potential data leakage and establishing standards for trustworthy assessment of LLMs in practical deployment scenarios.
